frameworks.name: bounded diffusion / drift-diffusion model
frameworks.one_line: Sequential sampling to a bound accounts for accuracy and decision report timing

frameworks.name: decision-action coupling
frameworks.one_line: Challenges view that categorical perceptual decisions precede motor actions

frameworks.name: working memory utility-based retention
frameworks.one_line: Sensory information held in memory for future categorization use

core_question: When perceptual decisions are uncoupled from the actions they bear upon, do humans postpone deliberation until response options are revealed and sample stored sensory evidence from memory?
key_assumptions: Random-dot motion evidence can be integrated over time toward a decision bound
Decision categorization can be dissociated from motor action specification
Stored sensory information can be re-accessed during a target-selection phase
manipulations: Delay period between motion stimulus and choice-target onset
Uncoupling of perceptual decision from response action via post-stimulus target reveal
key_findings: Decision accuracy varied across subjects, reflecting individual evidence-integration ability
Higher-accuracy subjects showed prolonged deliberation after target presentation
Decision report time depended on motion evidence strength
A bounded diffusion model with post-target sampling from memory fit the joint accuracy and timing pattern
interpretations: Subjects sequentially sample stored sensory information from memory during target selection rather than committing before targets appear
Working memory strategically retains information based on its future utility for categorization
